ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers soup noodle dishes broadly; no specific code exists for spicy tripe noodle soup. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of spicy tripe noodle soup, approximately 450 g, comprising wheat noodles, braised or flash-cooked beef tripe, and a spiced broth.

nutrient_source.title: Beef tripe (cooked) and wheat noodle soup — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Beef tripe, cooked (FDC ID 169451) and Noodle soup with meat, Chinese-style (NCC analogous entry); sodium adjusted for restaurant-style preparation.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; NCC Food and Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 香辣爆肚面 exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked beef tripe (FDC ID 169451) and Chinese-style noodle soup with meat, weighted at approximately 30% tripe, 35% noodles, 35% broth by mass. Sodium is elevated to reflect restaurant-style seasoning with soy sauce and chilli oil. Energy and macros are consistent with comparable Chinese noodle soup dishes in the NCC Food and Nutrient Database.

gi_source.title: Noodles, wheat, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to spicy tripe noodle soup dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 香辣爆肚面. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI ~52, low category). The tripe and broth components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as: (52 × 54.0 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 28.1. Available carbohydrate per serving = 12.0 g/100g × 450 g = 54.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, chilli oil, and seasoned broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 2160 mg per bowl), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles; portion size is the primary driver of postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size, as a standard 450 g bowl delivers an estimated glycemic load of 28.1 (high GL), despite the dish having a low glycemic index of 52; reducing noodle quantity and increasing tripe and vegetable content can lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium, moderate cholesterol from beef tripe, and saturated fat from chilli oil and tripe
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish with caution due to its high sodium content and moderate cholesterol contribution from beef tripe (approximately 55 mg cholesterol per 100 g), and should request reduced oil and soy sauce when ordering.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 香辣爆肚面 (Xiāng là bào dǔ miàn), translated as Spicy Tripe Noodle Soup, is a Northern Chinese noodle dish featuring flash-cooked or braised beef tripe (bào dǔ) served over wheat noodles in a richly spiced broth. The dish is associated with Northern Chinese culinary traditions, including influences from Xinjiang and Beijing-style offal cooking. The tripe is typically blanched or stir-fried at high heat to achieve a tender yet chewy texture, then combined with a broth seasoned with chilli oil, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, Sichuan peppercorn, and sesame oil. The flavour profile is bold, spicy, and savoury, with the characteristic aroma of toasted chilli and numbing spice from Sichuan pepper. In Singapore, this dish is served at Northern Chinese specialty restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from beef tripe, a meaningful source of vitamin B12 and zinc, with carbohydrate energy primarily from wheat noodles. The sodium content is high due to restaurant-style seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), though the glycemic load per standard bowl is high due to portion size, requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

ingredient_synonyms.爆肚: beef tripe, quickly blanched or stir-fried
ingredient_synonyms.chilli oil: là jiāo yóu, spiced red oil
ingredient_synonyms.wheat noodles: miàn tiáo, hand-pulled or machine-cut noodles

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 香辣爆肚面 was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked beef tripe and Chinese-style noodle soup with meat, weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct source match.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates; no direct laboratory analysis or approved-source exact match was available for this dish. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources used for this estimate. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA tripe entry used. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish type. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible in cooked beef tripe and wheat noodle preparations per USDA data. The dish is served at Bei Jiang Restaurant (北疆饭店) at Boat Quay, Singapore, as recorded in field survey data data. Halal status is unconfirmed as the restaurant's certification status is not verified in this record. GI category is low (GI = 52 ≤ 55) per Veritas threshold rules.
